Document [7] (Title: Informed Consent -- Issues of Concern -- Lack of Patient Comprehension) Tools such as the teach-back method or test/feedback method can be used to assess whether patients comprehend the risks, benefits, and alternatives of their treatment. The teach-back method can help both patients and clinicians concentrate on the essential aspects of the information. To encourage active patient participation, engagement techniques and open-ended questions may be used to gather insights into patients' needs and preferences. Patients should be encouraged to ask questions to foster a more interactive dialogue. Interactive media and graphical tools can improve shared decision-making and effectively assess and present risks. Future research should continue to explore interactive interventions, given that these methods appear superior in improving patient comprehension. [20] [21] [20]

Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2788) Amniotomy at delivery is an artificial rupture of the membranes (تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي), a deliberate obstetric procedure in which a clinician intentionally breaks the amniotic sac to accelerate labor. By creating a controlled opening, amniotomy facilitates the descent of the fetus, enhances the effectiveness of uterine contractions, and allows for more accurate fetal monitoring. The technique is typically employed when labor is progressing slowly, when there is a need to augment contractions, or to expedite delivery for maternal or fetal indications. While amniotomy can shorten the duration of labor and reduce the need for pharmacologic induction, it also carries potential risks such as infection, cord prolapse, or excessive fetal head compression, and therefore is performed after careful assessment of the clinical situation and informed consent. Proper selection of candidates and adherence to aseptic technique are essential to maximize the benefits of this artificial membrane rupture in obstetric care.
